title: 🎙️ Arts, Culture & Digital Storytelling
description: Today we explore how Cariboo and rural BC communities create, preserve, and share culture — from festivals and community radio to digital archives and open-source arts tools. We're looking for stories where creativity meets technology in small-town contexts, and where local artists and storytellers are finding new ways to reach audiences beyond geography. - Daily themed article selection for podcast generation

_podcast.theme_description: Today we explore how Cariboo and rural BC communities create, preserve, and share culture — from festivals and community radio to digital archives and open-source arts tools. We're looking for stories where creativity meets technology in small-town contexts, and where local artists and storytellers are finding new ways to reach audiences beyond geography.
_podcast.theme_scoring_prompt:
Rate how well this article fits the theme 'Arts, Culture & Digital Storytelling' — how Cariboo and rural BC communities create, preserve, and share culture.

CALIBRATION — use this ladder. Most genuine arts/culture/media journalism should land well above 0, even without a Cariboo tie-in:
- 80-100: Cariboo/Williams Lake-specific arts and culture news — community arts council grants or murals, local festivals, Indigenous film/storytelling, community theatre, museum/heritage programs, a Cariboo author or artist receiving an award, community radio or local publishing.
- 55-79: Rural BC or Canadian arts/culture journalism without a specific Cariboo tie — CBC Arts features on Indigenous storytelling, regional festivals, community media projects, heritage preservation, creative-economy stories in small towns, digital storytelling tools and platforms.
- 30-54: General arts, culture, media, or entertainment journalism with real creative/storytelling substance but no rural or community angle (national awards shows, mainstream film/TV/music industry coverage, publishing industry news).
- 15-29: Tangentially creative content (a tech feature that touches on media/publishing/streaming) or local Cariboo civic news with no arts/culture angle.
- 0-14: No arts, culture, media, or storytelling dimension at all.

Strong fits include: community arts journalism (arts councils, local festivals, heritage programs), oral history and cultural preservation projects, Indigenous film and storytelling, community theatre and local media, rural arts funding and the creative economy in small towns, and digital storytelling platforms and tools. Also strong fits: community media tech (podcasting, streaming, local publishing), creative tech economy, open-source arts tools, Cariboo arts festivals using digital outreach, animation/film/media production in rural BC, local radio/podcast infrastructure, and how rural communities use technology for creative expression. A story about a Cariboo community mural, a CBC Arts feature on BC Indigenous storytelling, a rural arts council receiving grants, or a Cariboo author/artist shortlisted for an award is a strong fit even without an explicit technology angle — cultural journalism matters to this audience. Penalise heavily (score 0–14): medical and health research with no cultural/storytelling angle; working lands and resource economy stories with no cultural framing; wildlife and nature stories without creative or community media context; data centre infrastructure and AI investment news; geopolitical conflict, war, and crime coverage; financial market and stock movement stories. Score 0-100 where 100 is a perfect thematic match and 0 is completely unrelated.

items.title: [Williams Lake Tribune] Garden and Art Tour adds colourful fun in Williams Lake
items.content_html:
🎨 ✏️

## Garden and Art Tour adds colourful fun in Williams Lake Published 12:33 pm Sunday, July 12, 2026 **By rebeccadyok** 1/10 Debra and Bob McNie on Pigeon Avenue were featured in this year's Williams Lake Garden and Art Tour. (Rebecca Dyok/Williams Lake Tribune) The weather held up in Williams Lake on Saturday, July 11 for a self-guided garden tour of local landscapes throughout the area complete with live music and local artwork. A total of 11 properties were available to explore in this year’s bi-annual Garden and Art Tour by the Williams Lake Garden Club with donations supporting Scout

items.title: [Williams Lake Tribune] 1,200 hectare wildfire burning west of Fraser Lake
items.content_html:
🎨 ✏️

## 1,200 hectare wildfire burning west of Fraser Lake Published 4:48 pm Sunday, July 12, 2026 **By Yashvika Grover** 1/2 K70748 increased fire behaviour on upslope areas of north flank July 10\. (BC Wildfire Service) A wildfire located 1.5 kilometres west of the Fraser River continues to grow. According to BC Wildfire Service (BCWS), the French Bar Creek fire (K70748) was first discovered on July 4 and the cause remains under investigation. The out-of-control blaze currently spans 1,246.3 hectares. BCWS has assigned 59 firefighters, 10 helicopters and 2 pieces of heavy equipment. BCWS said

items.title: [Boing Boing] UK politician Ann Widdecombe beaten to death in own home
items.content_html:
🎨 ✏️

British politician and novelist Ann Widdecombe, a former minister of prisons known for her dogmatic conservative views, was found dead Thursday at her home in Devon, England. Long out of parliament and 78 years old, it was initially assumed she had died naturally, but it soon emerged she was beaten to death and that police had arrested a 26 year-old man on suspicion of her murder. — Read the rest

The post UK politician Ann Widdecombe beaten to death in own home appeared first on Boing Boing.

items.content_html:
🎨 ✏️

A new study suggests spacecraft exhaust could quickly contaminate the moon's most scientifically valuable regions, potentially masking ancient clues about how life began on Earth. Researchers say future lunar missions should consider new ways to reduce and monitor this pollution before it becomes widespread.

items.title: [Boing Boing] A company tried to make Monopoly in America and couldn't get dice
items.content_html:
🎨 ✏️

When the WS Game Company got hit with a seven-figure tariff bill on the board games it imports from China, CEO Jonathan Silva decided to find out whether he could make one in America instead. He picked a 250th-birthday edition called Monopoly: The Americana Edition and, according to NPR, hit a wall almost immediately: he couldn't find anyone in the United States to make him 10,000 dice. — Read the rest

The post A company tried to make Monopoly in America and couldn't get dice appeared first on Boing Boing.

items.title: [Boing Boing] Passenger nearly pulled out a Ryanair window in mid-air
items.content_html:
🎨 ✏️

A window came off a Ryanair jet mid-flight over North Macedonia on Thursday, and a passenger was partly pulled through the hole before others hauled him back by his seat belt. The flight from Thessaloniki to Memmingen turned back after debris — Greek media said it came off an engine — struck and detached one of the cabin windows, decompressing the plane and dropping the oxygen masks. — Read the rest

The post Passenger nearly pulled out a Ryanair window in mid-air appeared first on Boing Boing.

items.title: [New Atlas] Ovaries take on a surprising new job after menopause, study reveals
items.content_html:
🎨 ✏️

The ovaries are the earliest aging organ. Their supply of egg-containing follicles slowly runs out over a woman's lifetime, so that by the time menopause arrives, follicle numbers have dwindled to approximately one thousand.

Continue Reading

Category: Biology, Science, Refractor: Science & Health

Tags: Menopause, ovary, Women, The Immune System

items.title: [ScienceDaily] Scientists finally solved the mystery of Earth's greatest mass extinction
items.content_html:
🎨 ✏️

Why do beaches today have seashells from clams and snails instead of brachiopods? A new study suggests the answer lies in Earth's greatest mass extinction, when warming oceans and falling oxygen levels wiped out animals that couldn't adapt. Species with body plans and metabolisms better suited to the changing conditions survived and went on to dominate the seas, offering a glimpse of how modern marine life could respond to climate change.

It’ll be interesting to see how and if those creative liberties Chalamet speaks of come into play in the film adaptation and what they mean for Chani, the Atreides family, and more.

items.content_html:
🎨 ✏️

items.title: [The Guardian Global Development] ‘If a woman is killed, they say she fell, she took poison’: Pakistan’s devastating rise in ‘hidden’ sexual violence
items.content_html:
🎨 ✏️

Karachi’s chief police surgeon condemns increasing acceptance of domestic murders and rapes of women and girls

A white-bearded man looks straight into the camera, in the video circulating for the past few weeks on social media. “I killed my wife,” he says calmly in Urdu. “We have a give-and-take arrangement and when she refused to give, I said I would take.”

Hours earlier, the 64-year-old had walked into a police station in Karachi’s Orangi neighbourhood and confessed to murdering Asma Begum, a 58-year-old mother of four, in the home they shared because she had refused him sex.

Continue reading...
